summary: Joel highlights a CSS fix shared by a Flickr developer that improves image scaling quality in Internet Explorer. The fix requires adding a non-standard CSS property to enable bicubic interpolation for image resizing. Joel points out that every other browser already uses bicubic interpolation by default, making IE's choice of a lower-quality default an obvious mistake. He uses this as a small example of poor default choices in software.
key_points: IE required a non-standard CSS extension (-ms-interpolation-mode:bicubic) to achieve smooth image scaling that other browsers provided by default
Choosing the wrong default setting can degrade user experience across an entire product, even when the correct behavior is technically supported
Browser inconsistencies force web developers to add workarounds for what should be standard behavior
money_quotes: All the other browsers use bicubic interpolation for scaling by default, because that's the only thing that make sense, but IE requires a non-standard CSS extension.
key_insight: Choosing poor defaults forces users and developers to work around problems that should never have existed in the first place.
